criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the two-step watermark forging pipeline: (i) estimating the watermark from a single watermarked image using a preference model, and (ii) pasting the estimated watermark onto a new image.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the preference model training, including the Bradley-Terry ranking loss, synthetic artifact generation, and adversarial perturbation scheme.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the watermark removal and forging procedure, including the gradient ascent optimization objective and the high-resolution rescaling strategy.

The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to check all implementation and evaluation details, including model architecture, training dataset, resolution, batch size, optimizer, learning rate, training steps, and GPU setup.

The agent has read Appendix C completely to check the Fourier artifact hyperparameters (N_max=50, r_max~U[60,200] for wave; s_min=20, s_max=50 for noise; M_max=10, s'_min=5, s'_max=35 for line style) and runtime details (SGD lr=0.05, k=50 to k=500 steps, input rescaled to 768x768).
The agent has read Table 1 to check the watermark forging results across CIN, MBRS, TrustMark, and Video Seal, with bit accuracy and PSNR metrics.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the watermark removal results across CIN, MBRS, TrustMark, and Video Seal watermarking methods.

The agent has read Table 3 to understand all ablation variants: binary cross-entropy loss, hinge loss, without adversarial perturbation, real watermarks as training data, and the full proposed method.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the contribution of each synthetic artifact type (wave only, noise only, line style only, all combined) to watermark removal performance.
The agent has read Tables 5 and 6 (Appendix B) to understand the effect of the number of backpropagation steps k (50, 100, 200, 300, 500) on watermark forging and removal performance.
The plan includes training a ConvNeXt V2-Tiny preference model from scratch on the SA-1b dataset using the Bradley-Terry ranking loss with procedurally generated synthetic artifacts.
The plan includes generating three types of Fourier-space synthetic artifacts (wave style, noise, line style) to create dispreferred image variants x- for preference model training.
The plan includes implementing adversarial perturbation of the negative samples x- during training, replacing every second batch's x- with an adversarially perturbed version.
The plan includes implementing the watermark forging and removal procedure via gradient ascent on the preference model score, with a fixed number of optimization steps k and a fixed learning rate.

The plan includes the high-resolution watermark forging strategy: resizing the input watermarked image to H×W, estimating the watermark at lower resolution, and resizing back to the original resolution before applying.
The plan includes evaluating watermark forging and removal on 100 SA-1b validation images watermarked by CIN, MBRS, TrustMark, and Video Seal using the same hidden message for all 100 images.
The plan includes comparing with all baseline methods: Warfare (n=1000), Image averaging (n=100), Noise blending (α=0.1), Gray image blending, DiffPure (FLUX.1 [dev]), CtrlRegen (step=0.1), and VAE.

The plan includes ablation experiments varying the training loss (ranking loss, binary cross-entropy, hinge loss), adversarial perturbation, training on real watermarks vs. procedural artifacts, and artifact type combinations.
The plan includes ablation experiments varying the number of backpropagation steps k (50, 100, 200, 300, 500) for both watermark forging and watermark removal.
The preference model is implemented as ConvNeXt V2-Tiny with a single scalar output R(x) ∈ ℝ, where higher values indicate preferred (cleaner) images.
The Bradley-Terry preference probability is implemented as Equation 1: p(x+ ≻ x-) = exp(R(x+)) / (exp(R(x+)) + exp(R(x-))).
The ranking loss is implemented as Equation 2: -E[(x+, x-)] [log σ(R(x+) - R(x-))], i.e., negative log-likelihood of the Bradley-Terry preference model.

The wave style artifact generation is implemented in Fourier space with non-zero amplitude at N~U{2,...,N_max} randomly sampled polar coordinates (r^(4/5)cos(θ), r^(4/5)sin(θ)) where r~U[0,r_max] and θ~U[0,2π].
The noise artifact generation is implemented in Fourier space with amplitude F(i,j)~U[0, exp(-||(i/σ², j/σ²)||_p)] where σ²~U[s_min, s_max] and p-norm with p = 4 - 3√(p'), p'~U[0,1].

The line style artifact generation is implemented in Fourier space with horizontal and vertical lines at positions l~N(0,ρ²) where M~U{3,...,M_max} and ρ~U[s'_min, s'_max], using the same number of vertical and horizontal lines.
The artifact generation randomly selects one of the three artifact types (wave, noise, line style) per image pair during training data generation.
The phase component of the Fourier spectrum is selected randomly for all artifact types, and the artifact is rescaled to the pixel-value range [-0.05, 0.05] after inverse Fourier transform.
The artifact diversity is increased by randomly sampling either RGB or grayscale artifacts for each training pair.
With 50% probability, the artifact ω is element-wise multiplied by a Just Noticeable Differences (JND) map to localize artifacts in high-frequency image regions only.

The training data pipeline loads images from SA-1b, resizes them to 768×768, generates synthetic artifact to create x-, then applies the same random image augmentation and random crop of size 256×256 to both x+ and x-.
The adversarial perturbation of negative samples is implemented as x̃- = x- + ε·∇_{x-} R(x-), using 2 gradient descent steps with learning rate randomly chosen from U[0.03, 0.09].
The adversarial training loop replaces the artifact-corrupted x- with the adversarially perturbed x̃- in every second batch to create the additional training pair (x+, x̃-).
The model is trained from scratch for 120k steps on 8 GPUs with a batch size of 16 per GPU using AdamW optimizer with a fixed learning rate of 1×10⁻⁵.
The watermark estimation is implemented as Equation 3: ŵ = argmax_δ R(x_w - δ), optimized via gradient ascent with a fixed number of steps k and fixed learning rate.
The watermark removal is implemented by computing xˆ = x_w - resize_ori(ŵ') where ŵ' is estimated from the resized watermarked image, consistent with the high-resolution strategy.

The watermark forging pipeline is implemented as Equation 4: y_ŵ = y + resize_ori(ŵ'), where ŵ' = argmax_δ R(resize_{H×W}(x_w) - δ), resizing the input watermarked image to H×W before estimating the watermark.
The watermark forging optimization uses SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.05 and input images rescaled to 768×768, as specified in Appendix C.
The Fourier artifact hyperparameters are implemented as specified in Appendix C: N_max=50, r_max~U[60,200] for wave style; s_min=20, s_max=50 for noise; M_max=10, s'_min=5, s'_max=35 for line style.

The evaluation code watermarks 100 SA-1b validation images per watermarking method (CIN, MBRS, TrustMark, Video Seal) using the same hidden message for all images, and measures bit accuracy of the respective watermark extractor.
The evaluation code computes PSNR with respect to the ground truth watermark-free image for both forging and removal evaluation.
The evaluation code implements all baseline methods for watermark forging: Warfare (n=1000), Image averaging (n=100), Noise blending (α=0.1), Gray image blending, and DiffPure (FLUX.1 [dev]).
The evaluation code implements all baseline methods for watermark removal: Image averaging (n=100), VAE (FLUX.1 [dev]), DiffPure (FLUX.1 [dev]), and CtrlRegen (step=0.1).
The ablation code implements binary cross-entropy loss training variant (two classes: positive x+ and negative x-) as an alternative to the ranking loss.
The ablation code implements the hinge loss training variant as an alternative to the ranking loss.
The ablation code implements the 'without perturbation' training variant, where no adversarial perturbation is applied to negative samples during training.

The ablation code implements the gray-box training variant using real watermark outputs from CIN, MBRS, TrustMark, and Video Seal as negative training samples x- instead of procedurally generated artifacts.
The ablation code for artifact type study independently trains models using only wave style, only noise, or only line style artifacts to evaluate each artifact type's contribution.
The ablation code for backpropagation steps evaluates watermark forging and removal with k ∈ {50, 100, 200, 300, 500} steps.
The preference model training code has been executed successfully for 120k steps on 8 GPUs without errors.

The watermark forging evaluation code (for all methods including the proposed approach with k=500 steps) has been executed successfully without errors on 100 SA-1b images for all four watermarking methods.

The watermark removal evaluation code (for all methods including the proposed approach with k=500 steps) has been executed successfully without errors on 100 SA-1b images for all four watermarking methods.
The ablation experiments on training loss (binary cross-entropy, hinge, ranking) and adversarial perturbation (with/without) have been executed successfully without errors.
The ablation experiments on artifact types (wave only, noise only, line style only, all combined) have been executed successfully for watermark removal evaluation.
The ablation experiments varying the number of backpropagation steps k (50, 100, 200, 300, 500) for watermark forging and removal have been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match Table 1 watermark forging results: the proposed method (n=1) achieves bit accuracy of 1.00 on CIN, 0.83 on MBRS, 0.61 on TrustMark, 0.83 on Video Seal, with PSNR of 31.3.
The execution outcomes match Table 2 watermark removal results: the proposed method achieves bit accuracy of 0.82 on CIN, 0.64 on MBRS, 0.60 on TrustMark, 0.49 on Video Seal, with PSNR of 31.2.

The execution outcomes confirm the finding from Table 1 and 2: the proposed method outperforms all forging baselines on Video Seal (bit acc. 0.83), where image averaging (n=100) fails (bit acc. 0.59), demonstrating the advantage of single-image forging for content-aware watermarking.

The execution outcomes match Table 3 ablation results: the binary cross-entropy variant achieves near-random forging performance (CIN=0.60, MBRS=0.53, TrustMark=0.52, Video Seal=0.47 bit acc.) confirming the ranking loss is essential.

The execution outcomes match Table 3 ablation results: the 'without perturbation' variant (CIN=0.97, MBRS=0.65, TrustMark=0.52, Video Seal=0.49 bit acc.) underperforms the full model, confirming the necessity of adversarial perturbation.

The execution outcomes match Table 3 ablation results: training on real watermarks (CIN=1.00, MBRS=0.67, TrustMark=0.58, Video Seal=0.77 bit acc.) performs worse than training on procedural artifacts (the full method), confirming procedural artifacts provide more diverse training signal.

The execution outcomes match Table 4 artifact type ablation for watermark removal: the wave-only model is most effective in removing watermarks (CIN=0.98, MBRS=0.83, TrustMark=0.55, Video Seal=0.45), while combining all types yields the best overall performance.
The execution outcomes match Tables 5 and 6: increasing k from 50 to 500 progressively improves forging/removal bit accuracy at the cost of lower PSNR, with k=500 achieving the reported main results.

The execution outcomes confirm that the proposed method produces higher PSNR than DiffPure (31.3 vs 26.6 for forging; 31.2 vs 25.4 for removal) while achieving comparable or better bit accuracy, confirming better image quality preservation.
